description: The mainstream Christian tradition tracing authority through apostolic succession and the rule of faith. Before Nicaea (325), this stream is called "proto-orthodox" by historians to distinguish it from the later formally defined orthodoxy.
modern_descendants: Eastern Orthodox; Roman Catholic; Oriental Orthodox

description: The mainstream tradition after Nicaea (325) defined by the Nicene Creed, Chalcedonian Christology, and the seven ecumenical councils. Split into Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic in 1054.

The earliest form of Christianity, maintaining Jewish law observance alongside faith in Jesus as Messiah. Centered in Jerusalem under James; scattered after 70 AD; marginalized after 135 AD when Gentile Christianity became dominant.
Teaching that the Son of God was a created being, not co-eternal with the Father. Condemned at Nicaea (325) but remained influential, especially among Germanic tribes, until the 7th century.

North African schism over whether bishops who surrendered scriptures during persecution could validly ordain. Insisted on moral purity of clergy for valid sacraments. Condemned at Arles (314) and Carthage (411).
Charismatic movement emphasizing new prophecy, strict asceticism, and imminent eschatology. Attracted Tertullian. Condemned by multiple synods but persisted in Asia Minor for centuries.
Diverse movement teaching salvation through secret knowledge (gnosis), dualistic cosmology, and a demiurge creator distinct from the supreme God. Refuted by Irenaeus, Tertullian, and Hippolytus.
Rejected the Old Testament and taught that the God of the OT was a different deity from the Father of Jesus. Created the first known Christian canon. Excommunicated from Rome c.144.
The Syriac-speaking church of Mesopotamia and Persia that rejected the Council of Ephesus (431) and the Theotokos title. Expanded dramatically along the Silk Road to China, India, and Central Asia.
modern_descendants: Assyrian Church of the East; Chaldean Catholic Church

description: Tradition rejecting the Council of Chalcedon (451), holding that Christ has one united nature (mia physis) that is both divine and human. "Monophysite" is considered a pejorative misnomer.
modern_descendants: Coptic Orthodox; Ethiopian Orthodox; Armenian Apostolic; Syriac Orthodox

description: Rigorist schism after the Decian persecution, insisting that Christians who lapsed during persecution could never be readmitted. Elected Novatian as rival bishop of Rome.
Teaching that humans can achieve salvation through their own free will without divine grace, and that original sin did not corrupt human nature. Condemned at Carthage (418) and Ephesus (431).

The Aramaic/Syriac-speaking Christian tradition centered on Edessa, Nisibis, and Antioch. Produced distinctive liturgy, hymnody (Ephrem), and theology. Split into East Syriac (Church of the East) and West Syriac (Syriac Orthodox) traditions.
modern_descendants: Syriac Orthodox Church; Maronite Church; Assyrian Church of the East
